blocks.text: The Why and The What - Biking across Europe
Hey everyone,
Family, friends or anyone else who's found this blog. I will be documenting my bike tour from Greece to Morocco.
Why am I doing this?

Two years ago during a small overnight bike trip to cultus lake, British Columbia. I became obsessed with the idea to bike across Europe. The original plan was Portugal to Russia. But obviously that quickly became impossible with the war in Ukraine.

Regarding bike touring, I had been inspired by seeing Adam who I am travelling with, bike across the United States during a two month period. And Taiga, someone I met at university, and Adams best friend, bike all over Japan and Europe for 10 euros a day.

The plan slowly developed over the last two years for me and Adam to start in Athens, Greece and adventure off the beaten path biking west to Morocco, with no exact route in mind and being open to changing plans on a whim.

I say "the plan" but the reality is we do not have one. I also say Greece to Morocco but during the first six days of this trip so far everything has been so unpredictable it's just as likely I end up in Iceland as Morocco. I realize I could easily spend the next three months biking around Greece alone. I will repeat myself a lot but it's out-of-this-world beautiful.

And finally for the rational of why travelling by bicycle. Biking is an interesting way to travel. You are vulnerable to everything. Cars, Dogs, weather. it takes a while to get anywhere when you're carrying 50kg of gear and equipment on your bike. But the best part is the sense of freedom on the bike is unmatched. I am carrying everything I need to survive (tent, credit card, stove, lots of water, etc) with me at all times. And I can go anywhere anytime I want. I just stop biking and set up my tent And so anything. It's amazing and it's ridiculously cheap. (Camping so far has been completely free)

Secondly, I think that to become a better person we always need to try and get out of our comfort zone to grow and learn. I have never camped alone. I have never cooked myself food camping, the longest trip I have done on a bike is one night. And here I am living on the road with my bike for three months. I will climb mountains, I will bike in the rain, I will bike in the blazing heat, I will be uncomfortable. But here I am.
Biking from Greece to Morocco sound impossible to me. And I just want see if I can do it.
Why am I now writing this?

I never would have thought I would be writing about this trip for others. I am not a writer all, i find it difficult, and I wanted to keep this trip private. I felt no need to share this with anyone whiles its happening, and instead just tell stories afterwards to friends and family. Then Greece happened. My first five days in Greece have completely shocked me. I am in love with Greece and bike touring. Each day so far I have told myself. "This is the most beautiful place I have ever been". Or "These are the most beautiful women I have ever seen" haha. It just keeps getting better day after day.

I have come to the conclusion that I want friends and family to share this experience and "come with me" on this tour. Hopefully what I will write will be interesting, entertaining, or inspiring to someone. We can all share the struggles and the really awesome things that will come in the next three and a half months on the road.

Each day I'll try and post a blog a photo of things that are happening where I'm going to a meeting. I've been in Greece for five days now, and I've decided I want to share this with friends and family. Greece is just a magical place. And I'm very excited to share all my experiences with anyone reading this. This blog won't be pretty, It won't be perfect, I'm a pretty mediocre writer. But I hope you enjoy following along with me.
I'm starting this blog five days into my journey so here's the first two days so far. I'll be a bit behind on writing these but I'll try and catch up.😉
Day 0,1 and 2
(We lost a day in timezone changes)

blocks.caption: Athens airport to Vari to Athens along the coast

blocks.text: I was flying from Vancouver through London to Athens in Greece. To be honest, I didn't plan anything of this trip and I didn't really know anything of Athens. I had no idea what Greece looks like. What Athens looks like. I found out what the Acropolis is in a conversation on the flight to London (If you don't know it's a really important Greek ruin). Before a few days ago I could not tell you anything about Greece or Greek history except that I don't like Greek salad. And I had no idea where I was going from the Athens airport, or to a place to sleep when I arrived. Sounds fun, right? The first day was been travelling. In Vancouver, I packed my bike into a box. Put about 1000 layers of bubble wrap around my bike. I check my bike into my British Airways flight. My friend Dominic helped me a lot getting the bike in the box. (Thanks bro 😎).
I was terrified of flying with my bike. I really common problem is that a bike gets damaged in transit. That wouldn't be a good way to start my trip.

14 hours later, I arrived in Athens airport Greece at 3 am. I feel so much excitement on the plane ride, that I couldn't sleep, even so I landed at 3 AM with so much energy. Adam and I have decided on the flight that the first time as us we wouldn't sleep at all.
Arriving in Athens was awesome, the Greek letters looks so cool. I was taking pictures of every sign. Just cause I love the Greek letters so much.
We go to the baggage claim, will be about 10 minutes then we saw our bikes. Thankfully nothing was damaged!
At 3 am Greek time, We reassembled our bikes together. By 5 AM our bikes were successful assembled, and we hit the road!
And of course we left 30° C in Vancouver for 20° and raining and when we landed in Athens.
Our first destination was this little town outside of Athens, called Vari. We just randomly selected on the map because it had a beach. And we wanted a post plane shower.

The bike ride from the airport to Vari was about 35 km. The roads were terrible. Not the actual roads themselves but the giant trucks and narrow road shoulders. We were taking the highway. I was pleasantly surprised as the level of driving. Mostly was really kind to us and passing us in their cars slowly rather than speeding by.

At one point we peeled off the highway to take a quieter smaller road. Only to be met by hundreds of guard dogs, lucky the owners had fences, expect for the pick of sheep dogs we met on the road. They all looked like they want to eat our bicycles.

blocks.caption: we saw many dogs who wanted us for breakfast

blocks.text: Eventually we landed in Vari, and both took her first plunge into the Mediterranean Sea. The waters only slightly warmer than Vancouver right now 🥹. But it was great going for a swim.

We then proceeded along the coast to Athens, the main city, biking, along to Seaside Highway. With a stunning view of houses and buildings built into the Mountainside, and massive yachts in the marina.

During the plane ride, we reserved a hostel in the centre of Athens. Zorbas hostel, 11 euros per night. About 2 km from the centre of Athens. Biking to the hospital from Vari, was not so easy. Adam got two flat tires, and biking in the city of Athens is completely mayhem. The highways are filled with truck, drivers fast cars with no bike lanes. And the small side streets are like mazes. I had to check the map every two street to not get lost. The last 3 km of our trip to the hostel, took an hour of biking (or rather an hour of making wrong turns on one way streets).

blocks.caption: Vari Village and a swim in the Mediterranean

blocks.text: When we finally arrived at Zorbas Hostal. I was pretty sure I had minor heatstroke since the previous rain had turned in 27 degrees and sunny. For 11 euros a night the hostel was awesome. The first thing I did was head to the bed and take a short nap.
After waking up it was about 5 pm and we walked around Greece, towards to city center. Here are some of my first im professions of Athens.

items: All the building and streets have amazing and unique architecture. They also have flowers all over the place. It also seems like every two blocks the style of all the building changes.

The Athenians are kind, however the level of English for people over the age of 35 is very low, and you could tell also some Greek people we met were fed up with tourists. Usually saying "efharisto" (Thank you in greek) a million times was enough to make even the coldest Athenian smile.

blocks.text: Still reading? A lot happened this day. Instead of sleeping in the morning we were biking.
On the post nap walk we took the metro in Greece, even the subways system looked so different then what I was used it. It took a while to figure out how to get the ticket for the metro.

We then went to a view point in Athens. The Acropolis! This is some really important historial thing in Greece. It cost us 10 euro to get in. Lots of call columns and impressive engineering. I couldn't help but wonder what this looked like back in the day.

It was also here that I realized just how big Athens is. I then understood why it took so long to get from the coast to the hostel, from the view point the city seemed like an endless sea of houses and building. Adam and I looked it up and Athens has a population density triple of Vancouver and 5 million people.
After acropolis we got a beer then wet back to the hostel and got an early nights sleep.
Day 3 Saturday 20th
We spent day two walking around the streets of Athens. We talked to the front desk guy in the hostel. He circled four neighbours on a purple map and we walked to each one.

Walking through Athens was a much more peaceful experience than biking through Athens. All the roads, packed with cars we biked on, has great sidewalks. Here are some interesting things about walking in Athens

items: The crosswalks - in Vancouver you have so much time to walk across the road. It's designed so that even the slowest snail can cross the street in time. In Athens they are FAST. We basically to get across the road. They are built for speed. They start the count not telling you how it's much time you you have to cross. But rather a countdown for then the pedestrian needs to start sprinting across the street.
Ancient Ruins are nestled into the town. Shops and Ancient Greek libraries are side by side.
There are so many tall marble columns. I have seen enough columns that I could probably build one now.
There are no traffic laws. okay maybe there are some - but we saw motorbikes driving through seating areas of restaurants, and cars parked on meridian road dividers and no one would even bat an eye.

blocks.text: Saw where the first Olympic Games was held. That was neat.

After the city walk and chilling on some rocks and listening to music which a view of the city and Parthenon. We went back to the hostel, went to an afghani restaurant and eat all you could eat Afghan food for 6 euro. And started drinking.

Had a fun night out in Athens downtown with some other travellers from the Hostel. Bars in Athens don't close till 5 am :). Great night out, and some great conversations with great people. Great great great.
There's the first two days. I wrote this on day six so tomorrow I'll post again my travels from day 3 and 4.
Okay so....
Every single day so far has been absolutely incredible and I can't wait to share more.

blocks.caption: beach side hot springs and our campsite last night

blocks.text: I am currently writing this inside of a natural hot spring, heated by a 700m high volcano, on the Greek island of Methena, a small island with 1000 people living on it. The hot spring is located right beside the ocean with an incredible view of some other Greek islands and the Peloponnese mountain ranges (which we will be headed to shortly). There is no one else but us, and we camped here last night. But this post is getting really long so I'll leave day 4 and 5 for tomorrow.
Hope you enjoyed post 1.
Let the adventures begin.
With Love,
- Erik
